title: Tischchen deck dich, Goldesel, und Knüppel aus dem Sack
titleEn: The Wishing-Table, the Gold-Ass, and the Cudgel in the Sack
part: The Wishing-Table, the Gold-Ass, and the Cudgel in the Sack
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 36. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: KHM 36, the last of the Grimms' three-brothers tales, joining two folk motifs: the lying goat that gets each of a tailor's sons beaten and cast out, and the three magic gifts — a table that lays itself with food, an ass that spits gold, and a cudgel in a sack — of which a thieving innkeeper robs the first two brothers until the third's cudgel wins them back. The text is the 1857 seventh edition, spelling preserved exactly. Among the older forms you meet here are the -ie- spellings gieng, fieng, hieng, empfieng, weggieng and giengen; th-words such as Thier, Thierlein, thun/gethan, Wirth, Wirthshaus, Wirthschaft, Hausthüre, Stallthüre, Muthe and Rothe; the ß of mußte, faßte and verschloß; and Ducaten, Tact and theurer for modern Dukaten, Takt and teurer.

sentences.t: Vor Zeiten war ein Schneider, der drei Söhne hatte und nur eine einzige Ziege.
sentences.l: In former times there was a tailor, who had three sons and only one single nanny-goat.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a tailor who had three sons and but a single nanny-goat.
sentences.n: “Vor Zeiten” = in olden days, in times past — a storyteller's opening. This one goat, kept alive by three sons in turn, is the hinge on which the whole tale swings.

sentences.t: Aber die Ziege, weil sie alle zusammen mit ihrer Milch ernährte, mußte ihr gutes Futter haben und täglich hinaus auf die Weide geführt werden.
sentences.l: But the goat, because she nourished them all together with her milk, had to have her good fodder and be led out daily onto the pasture.
sentences.i: But the goat, since she fed the whole household with her milk, had to have good fodder and be taken out to pasture every day.
sentences.n: mußte keeps the 1857 ß (modern musste). “geführt werden” is a passive infinitive (“to be led”); German piles the auxiliary werden at the very end.

sentences.t: Die Söhne thaten das auch nach der Reihe.
sentences.l: The sons did that too in turn.
sentences.i: And the sons did so, each taking his turn.
sentences.n: thaten is the 1857 spelling of taten (did), from thun = modern tun; the th- will recur (Thier, gethan). “nach der Reihe” = in turn, by rotation — a phrase the tale leans on.

sentences.t: Einmal brachte sie der älteste auf den Kirchhof, wo die schönsten Kräuter standen, ließ sie da fressen und herumspringen.
sentences.l: Once the eldest brought her onto the churchyard, where the finest herbs stood, let her feed there and jump about.
sentences.i: One time the eldest took her to the churchyard, where the finest greens grew, and let her graze and skip about there.
sentences.n: fressen is the verb for an animal's eating (greedy, of beasts), against essen for people. ließ = let/allowed (from lassen), governing the two bare infinitives fressen and herumspringen.

sentences.t: Abends, als es Zeit war heim zu gehen, fragte er „Ziege, bist du satt?“
sentences.l: In the evening, when it was time to go home, he asked “goat, are you full?”
sentences.i: In the evening, when it was time to go home, he asked: “Goat, are you full?”
sentences.n: satt = full, sated — the key word of the goat's two songs. This question, and the goat's answer, form the tale's opening refrain-pattern, repeated with each son.

sentences.t: Die Ziege antwortete
sentences.l: The goat answered
sentences.i: The goat answered:
sentences.n: The sentence breaks off deliberately — the little verse that follows completes it.

sentences.t:
„ich bin so satt,
ich mag kein Blatt: meh! meh!“
sentences.l:
“I am so full,
I want no leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.i: “I'm so full, I won't touch a leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.n: The goat's satisfied song, which she sings to whoever brings her home — four times in this tale. meh! meh! is her bleat (English “baa”). The rhyme satt / Blatt (full / leaf) drives it.

sentences.t: „So komm nach Haus“ sprach der Junge, faßte sie am Strickchen, führte sie in den Stall und band sie fest.
sentences.l: “So come home” spoke the boy, took hold of her by the little-rope, led her into the stall and tied her fast.
sentences.i: “Then come home,” said the boy, caught her by the tether, led her into the stall and tied her up.
sentences.n: faßte keeps the 1857 ß (modern fasste). Strickchen is a diminutive of Strick (rope) — a little lead or tether; Grimm's -chen/-lein diminutives are everywhere.

sentences.t: „Nun,“ sagte der alte Schneider, „hat die Ziege ihr gehöriges Futter?“
sentences.l: “Now,” said the old tailor, “has the goat her proper fodder?”
sentences.i: “Well,” said the old tailor, “has the goat had her proper feed?”
sentences.n: “gehöriges Futter” = the fodder due to her, her rightful feed. This exchange between father and son repeats almost word for word three times.

sentences.t: „O,“ antwortete der Sohn, „die ist so satt, sie mag kein Blatt.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” answered the son, “she is so full, she wants no leaf.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” answered the son, “she's so full she won't touch a leaf.”
sentences.n: The son echoes the goat's own rhyme (satt / Blatt) as his reassurance. die here = “she” (a demonstrative pointing back to the goat). mag = wants, cares for (from mögen).

sentences.t: Der Vater aber wollte sich selbst überzeugen, gieng hinab in den Stall, streichelte das liebe Thier und fragte „Ziege, bist du auch satt?“
sentences.l: The father however wanted to convince himself, went down into the stall, stroked the dear beast and asked “goat, are you really full?”
sentences.i: But the father wanted to see for himself, went down to the stall, stroked the dear creature and asked: “Goat, are you really full?”
sentences.n: “sich überzeugen” = to satisfy oneself, see for oneself (reflexive). gieng is the 1857 spelling of ging (went) — the old -ie- runs through the tale (fieng, empfieng, hieng). Thier = modern Tier (animal). auch here nudges the question: “are you truly full?”

sentences.t: Die Ziege antwortete
sentences.l: The goat answered
sentences.i: The goat answered:

sentences.t:
„wovon sollt ich satt sein?
ich sprang nur über Gräbelein,
und fand kein einzig Blättelein: meh! meh!“
sentences.l:
“from what should I be full?
I sprang only over little-graves,
and found not a single little-leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.i: “How was I to get full? / I only leapt over the little graves / and never found a single leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.n: The goat's lying counter-song, sung to the father — it exposes the son as a liar. wovon = “from what, whereof.” Gräbelein and Blättelein are diminutives (little graves, little leaf); a later verse spells the leaf Blättlein. sollt is the old short form of sollte (should).

sentences.t: „Was muß ich hören!“ rief der Schneider, lief hinauf und sprach zu dem Jungen „ei, du Lügner, sagst die Ziege wäre satt, und hast sie hungern lassen?“
sentences.l: “What must I hear!” cried the tailor, ran up and spoke to the boy “ah, you liar, you say the goat were full, and have let her go hungry?”
sentences.i: “What must I hear!” cried the tailor, ran upstairs and said to the boy: “Ah, you liar — you say the goat is full, and you've let her go hungry?”
sentences.n: muß keeps the 1857 ß (modern muss). “die Ziege wäre satt” uses the subjunctive wäre for reported speech — German marks what is merely claimed (not the narrator's fact) this way; you will meet it constantly. hinauflaufen (to run up) is separable: “lief hinauf.”

sentences.t: und in seinem Zorne nahm er die Elle von der Wand und jagte ihn mit Schlägen hinaus.
sentences.l: and in his wrath he took the yard-stick from the wall and drove him out with blows.
sentences.i: and in his rage he took the yardstick from the wall and drove him out with a beating.
sentences.n: die Elle is a tailor's ell or yard-measure — his everyday tool, here his weapon; it recurs as the father's stick each time. “im Zorne” keeps an old dative -e (Zorne).

sentences.t: Am andern Tag war die Reihe am zweiten Sohn, der suchte an der Gartenhecke einen Platz aus, wo lauter gute Kräuter standen, und die Ziege fraß sie rein ab.
sentences.l: On the next day the turn was at the second son, who picked out a place by the garden hedge, where nothing but good herbs stood, and the goat grazed them clean off.
sentences.i: The next day it was the second son's turn; he picked out a spot by the garden hedge where there was nothing but good greens, and the goat cropped them bare.
sentences.n: “die Reihe war an…” = it was (someone's) turn. lauter here = nothing but, sheer. “abfressen” (to graze off) is separable: “fraß sie rein ab”; rein = clean, entirely.

sentences.t: Abends, als er heim wollte, fragte er „Ziege, bist du satt?“
sentences.l: In the evening, when he wanted to go home, he asked “goat, are you full?”
sentences.i: In the evening, when he wanted to head home, he asked: “Goat, are you full?”
sentences.n: “heim wollte” — with verbs of motion German often drops the infinitive after wollen: “wanted [to go] home.”

sentences.t: Die Ziege antwortete
sentences.l: The goat answered
sentences.i: The goat answered:

sentences.t:
„ich bin so satt,
ich mag kein Blatt: meh! meh!“
sentences.l:
“I am so full,
I want no leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.i: “I'm so full, I won't touch a leaf: baa! baa!”

sentences.t: „So komm nach Haus,“ sprach der Junge, zog sie heim und band sie im Stalle fest.
sentences.l: “So come home,” spoke the boy, drew her home and tied her fast in the stall.
sentences.i: “Then come home,” said the boy, led her home and tied her up in the stall.
sentences.n: “heimziehen” (to draw/lead home) is separable: “zog sie heim.” im Stalle keeps the old dative -e (Stalle for Stall).

sentences.t: „Nun,“ sagte der alte Schneider, „hat die Ziege ihr gehöriges Futter?“
sentences.l: “Now,” said the old tailor, “has the goat her proper fodder?”
sentences.i: “Well,” said the old tailor, “has the goat had her proper feed?”

sentences.t: „O,“ antwortete der Sohn, „die ist so satt, sie mag kein Blatt.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” answered the son, “she is so full, she wants no leaf.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” answered the son, “she's so full she won't touch a leaf.”

sentences.t: Der Schneider wollte sich darauf nicht verlassen, gieng hinab in den Stall und fragte „Ziege, bist du auch satt?“
sentences.l: The tailor wanted not to rely on that, went down into the stall and asked “goat, are you really full?”
sentences.i: The tailor would not take his word for it, went down to the stall and asked: “Goat, are you really full?”
sentences.n: “sich auf etwas verlassen” = to rely/depend on something (reflexive).

sentences.t: Die Ziege antwortete
sentences.l: The goat answered
sentences.i: The goat answered:

sentences.t:
„wovon sollt ich satt sein?
ich sprang nur über Gräbelein,
und fand kein einzig Blättelein: meh! meh!“
sentences.l:
“from what should I be full?
I sprang only over little-graves,
and found not a single little-leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.i: “How was I to get full? / I only leapt over the little graves / and never found a single leaf: baa! baa!”

sentences.t: „Der gottlose Bösewicht!“ schrie der Schneider, „so ein frommes Thier hungern zu lassen!“ lief hinauf, und schlug mit der Elle den Jungen zur Hausthüre hinaus.
sentences.l: “The godless villain!” screamed the tailor, “to let such a pious beast go hungry!” ran up, and struck the boy out with the ell to the house-door.
sentences.i: “The godless scoundrel!” screamed the tailor, “to let such a gentle creature starve!” — ran upstairs and beat the boy out the front door with the yardstick.
sentences.n: gottlos = godless, wicked; fromm here of an animal = gentle, good. Hausthüre = modern Haustür (front door); the th- is 1857 (also Thier, thun). “zur Hausthüre hinaus” = out the door.

sentences.t: Die Reihe kam jetzt an den dritten Sohn, der wollte seine Sache gut machen, suchte Buschwerk mit dem schönsten Laube aus, und ließ die Ziege daran fressen.
sentences.l: The turn came now to the third son, who wanted to do his business well, picked out brushwood with the finest foliage, and let the goat feed on it.
sentences.i: Now it was the third son's turn; he meant to do the thing right, sought out bushes with the finest leaves, and let the goat feed on them.
sentences.n: “seine Sache gut machen” = to do one's job well, acquit oneself well. “aussuchen” (to pick out) is separable: “suchte… aus.” daran = on it, at it.

sentences.t: Abends, als er heim wollte, fragte er „Ziege, bist du auch satt?“
sentences.l: In the evening, when he wanted to go home, he asked “goat, are you really full?”
sentences.i: In the evening, when he wanted to head home, he asked: “Goat, are you really full?”

sentences.t: Die Ziege antwortete
sentences.l: The goat answered
sentences.i: The goat answered:

sentences.t:
„ich bin so satt,
ich mag kein Blatt: meh! meh!“
sentences.l:
“I am so full,
I want no leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.i: “I'm so full, I won't touch a leaf: baa! baa!”

sentences.t: „So komm nach Haus,“ sagte der Junge, führte sie in den Stall und band sie fest.
sentences.l: “So come home,” said the boy, led her into the stall and tied her fast.
sentences.i: “Then come home,” said the boy, led her into the stall and tied her up.

sentences.t: „Nun,“ sagte der alte Schneider, „hat die Ziege ihr gehöriges Futter?“
sentences.l: “Now,” said the old tailor, “has the goat her proper fodder?”
sentences.i: “Well,” said the old tailor, “has the goat had her proper feed?”

sentences.t: „O,“ antwortete der Sohn, „die ist so satt, sie mag kein Blatt.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” answered the son, “she is so full, she wants no leaf.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” answered the son, “she's so full she won't touch a leaf.”

sentences.t: Der Schneider traute nicht, gieng hinab und fragte „Ziege, bist du auch satt?“
sentences.l: The tailor did not trust it, went down and asked “goat, are you really full?”
sentences.i: The tailor did not trust it, went down and asked: “Goat, are you really full?”
sentences.n: trauen = to trust (here with no object: he trusts it not).

sentences.t: Das boshafte Thier antwortete
sentences.l: The malicious beast answered
sentences.i: The spiteful creature answered:
sentences.n: boshaft = malicious, spiteful — the narrator now names the goat's character outright, having let the pattern speak for itself twice.

sentences.t:
„wovon sollt ich satt sein?
ich sprang nur über Gräbelein,
und fand kein einzig Blättlein: meh! meh!“
sentences.l:
“from what should I be full?
I sprang only over little-graves,
and found not a single little-leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.i: “How was I to get full? / I only leapt over the little graves / and never found a single leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.n: Same lying song, now spelled Blättlein rather than Blättelein — Grimm's diminutives were not fixed. The lie has now cost all three sons.

sentences.t: „O die Lügenbrut!“ rief der Schneider, „einer so gottlos und pflichtvergessen wie der andere! ihr sollt mich nicht länger zum Narren haben!“
sentences.l: “O the lie-brood!” cried the tailor, “one as godless and duty-forgetful as the other! you shall not have me for a fool any longer!”
sentences.i: “Oh, you pack of liars!” cried the tailor, “one as godless and heedless of duty as the next! You'll make a fool of me no longer!”
sentences.n: Lügenbrut = brood/pack of liars, an insult. “zum Narren haben” = to make a fool of. He is cursing all three sons at once (einer… wie der andere = one like the other).

sentences.t: und vor Zorn ganz außer sich sprang er hinauf und gerbte dem armen Jungen mit der Elle den Rücken so gewaltig, daß er zum Haus hinaus sprang.
sentences.l: and beside himself with wrath he sprang up and tanned the poor boy's back with the ell so mightily, that he sprang out of the house.
sentences.i: and, quite beside himself with rage, he leapt up and gave the poor boy's back such a tanning with the yardstick that the boy leapt out of the house.
sentences.n: “jemandem den Rücken gerben” — literally “to tan someone's back,” i.e. to thrash him soundly; the tailor's trade (leather, cloth) colours the metaphor. daß (1857 for dass) here is a result-clause: “so mightily that…”. “dem… Jungen den Rücken” is dative-of-possession: “the boy's back.”

sentences.t: Der alte Schneider war nun mit seiner Ziege allein.
sentences.l: The old tailor was now alone with his goat.
sentences.n: The turning point: with all three sons gone, the tailor is left with the very cause of it.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen gieng er hinab in den Stall, liebkoste die Ziege und sprach „komm, mein liebes Thierlein, ich will dich selbst zur Weide führen.“
sentences.l: On the next morning he went down into the stall, caressed the goat and spoke “come, my dear little-beast, I will lead you to pasture myself.”
sentences.i: The next morning he went down to the stall, fondled the goat and said: “Come, my dear little creature, I'll take you to pasture myself.”
sentences.n: Thierlein is a tender diminutive of Thier (little animal). “ich will dich selbst… führen” — will here = intend/mean to, not English “will.”

sentences.t: Er nahm sie am Strick und brachte sie zu grünen Hecken und unter Schafrippe und was sonst die Ziegen gerne fressen.
sentences.l: He took her by the rope and brought her to green hedges and among sheep's-rib and whatever else the goats gladly eat.
sentences.i: He took her by the rope and brought her to green hedges and sheep's-rib and whatever else goats like to eat.
sentences.n: Schafrippe (“sheep's-rib”) is a fodder plant, a folk name. gerne = gladly, willingly (goats “like to” eat it).

sentences.t: „Da kannst du dich einmal nach Herzenslust sättigen“ sprach er zu ihr, und ließ sie weiden bis zum Abend.
sentences.l: “There you can for once sate yourself to your heart's content” spoke he to her, and let her graze until the evening.
sentences.i: “There you can eat your fill for once, to your heart's content,” he said to her, and let her graze until evening.
sentences.n: “sich sättigen” = to eat one's fill (reflexive). “nach Herzenslust” = to one's heart's content — a set phrase. einmal here = for once.

sentences.t: Da fragte er „Ziege, bist du satt?“
sentences.l: Then he asked “goat, are you full?”
sentences.i: Then he asked: “Goat, are you full?”

sentences.t: Sie antwortete
sentences.l: She answered
sentences.i: She answered:

sentences.t:
„ich bin so satt,
ich mag kein Blatt: meh! meh!“
sentences.l:
“I am so full,
I want no leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.i: “I'm so full, I won't touch a leaf: baa! baa!”

sentences.t: „So komm nach Haus“ sagte der Schneider, führte sie in den Stall und band sie fest.
sentences.l: “So come home” said the tailor, led her into the stall and tied her fast.
sentences.i: “Then come home,” said the tailor, led her into the stall and tied her up.

sentences.t: Als er weggieng, kehrte er sich noch einmal um, und sagte „nun bist du doch einmal satt!“
sentences.l: When he went away, he turned himself around once more, and said “now you are for once full after all!”
sentences.i: As he was leaving, he turned round once more and said: “Now at last you're full!”
sentences.n: “umkehren” / “sich umkehren” here = to turn round; weggehen (to go off) is separable, in the 1857 spelling weggieng. doch and einmal together give a satisfied “now surely, at last.”

sentences.t: Aber die Ziege machte es ihm nicht besser und rief
sentences.l: But the goat made it no better for him and called
sentences.i: But the goat treated him no better, and cried:
sentences.n: “machte es ihm nicht besser” = did him no better turn (than she had the sons). The sentence breaks off into the coming verse.

sentences.t:
„wie sollt ich satt sein?
ich sprang nur über Gräbelein,
und fand kein einzig Blättlein: meh! meh!“
sentences.l:
“how should I be full?
I sprang only over little-graves,
and found not a single little-leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.i: “How was I to get full? / I only leapt over the little graves / and never found a single leaf: baa! baa!”
sentences.n: This time she opens with wie sollt (“how should I be full?”) instead of wovon — the same lie, now told to the father's own face. It is what finally undeceives him.

sentences.t: Als der Schneider das hörte, stutzte er und sah wohl daß er seine drei Söhne ohne Ursache verstoßen hatte.
sentences.l: When the tailor heard that, he stopped short and saw well that he had cast out his three sons without cause.
sentences.i: When the tailor heard that, he was taken aback and saw plainly that he had driven out his three sons for nothing.
sentences.n: stutzen = to stop short, be pulled up startled. wohl here = well, indeed (an intensifier, not “probably”). “ohne Ursache” = without cause. verstoßen = to cast out, disown.

sentences.t: „Wart,“ rief er, „du undankbares Geschöpf, dich fortzujagen ist noch zu wenig, ich will dich zeichnen daß du dich unter ehrbaren Schneidern nicht mehr darfst sehen lassen.“
sentences.l: “Wait,” cried he, “you ungrateful creature, to chase you off is still too little, I will mark you so that you may no longer let yourself be seen among honorable tailors.”
sentences.i: “Just wait,” he cried, “you ungrateful creature — driving you off is too little; I'll mark you so you can never show your face among honest tailors again.”
sentences.n: Wart = clipped imperative of warten (wait!). fortjagen (to chase off) here as a zu-infinitive noun-clause (“to chase you off”). zeichnen = to mark, brand. “sich sehen lassen” = to show oneself, be seen; darfst = may, is allowed.

sentences.t: In einer Hast sprang er hinauf, holte sein Bartmesser, seifte der Ziege den Kopf ein, und schor sie so glatt wie seine flache Hand.
sentences.l: In a haste he sprang up, fetched his razor, soaped the goat's head, and shore her as smooth as his flat hand.
sentences.i: In a rush he leapt up, fetched his razor, lathered the goat's head, and shaved her as smooth as the palm of his hand.
sentences.n: Bartmesser = a razor (beard-knife). “einseifen” (to soap up) is separable: “seifte… ein”; “der Ziege den Kopf” is dative-of-possession (the goat's head). schor is the past of scheren (to shear, shave). The shaving is what will finally expose the goat.

sentences.t: Und weil die Elle zu ehrenvoll gewesen wäre, holte er die Peitsche und versetzte ihr solche Hiebe, daß sie in gewaltigen Sprüngen davon lief.
sentences.l: And because the ell would have been too honorable, he fetched the whip and dealt her such blows, that she ran off in mighty leaps.
sentences.i: And because the yardstick would have been too honourable, he fetched the whip and dealt her such blows that she bolted off in great bounds.
sentences.n: “gewesen wäre” is subjunctive (would have been) — the ell is his trade's honest tool, too good to waste on her. “jemandem Hiebe versetzen” = to land/deal someone blows. daß here is again a result-clause.

sentences.t: Der Schneider, als er so ganz einsam in seinem Hause saß, verfiel in große Traurigkeit und hätte seine Söhne gerne wieder gehabt, aber niemand wußte wo sie hingerathen waren.
sentences.l: The tailor, as he sat so quite lonely in his house, fell into great sadness and would gladly have had his sons back, but nobody knew where they had got to.
sentences.i: The tailor, sitting all alone in his house, fell into deep sadness and would gladly have had his sons back, but no one knew where they had wandered off to.
sentences.n: “in Traurigkeit verfallen” = to sink into sorrow. “hätte… gerne… gehabt” is subjunctive: would have liked to have. hingerathen (modern hingeraten) = to have ended up, got to; wußte keeps the 1857 ß.

sentences.t: Der älteste war zu einem Schreiner in die Lehre gegangen, da lernte er fleißig und unverdrossen, und als seine Zeit herum war, daß er wandern sollte, schenkte ihm der Meister ein Tischchen, das gar kein besonderes Ansehen hatte und von gewöhnlichem Holz war: aber es hatte eine gute Eigenschaft.
sentences.l: The eldest had gone into apprenticeship with a joiner, there he learned diligently and untiring, and when his time was up, that he should go journeying, the master gave him a little table, which had no special look at all and was of ordinary wood: but it had a good quality.
sentences.i: The eldest had apprenticed himself to a joiner; there he learned diligently and without flagging, and when his time was up and he was to set out on his travels, the master gave him a little table that looked nothing special and was of plain wood — but it had a good property.
sentences.n: “in die Lehre gehen” = to enter apprenticeship; wandern here = a journeyman's travels (the Wanderjahre after training). schenken = to give as a gift. This little Tischchen is the first of the three magic gifts, one per son.

sentences.t: Wenn man es hinstellte, und sprach „Tischchen, deck dich,“ so war das gute Tischchen auf einmal mit einem saubern Tüchlein bedeckt, und stand da ein Teller, und Messer und Gabel daneben, und Schüsseln mit Gesottenem und Gebratenem, so viel Platz hatten, und ein großes Glas mit rothem Wein leuchtete daß einem das Herz lachte.
sentences.l: When one set it down, and spoke “little-table, cover yourself,” then the good little table was all at once covered with a clean little-cloth, and there stood a plate, and knife and fork beside it, and dishes with boiled and roasted meat, as many as had room, and a great glass with red wine gleamed so that one's heart laughed.
sentences.i: When you set it down and said “Table, set yourself,” at once the good little table was spread with a clean cloth, and there stood a plate, with knife and fork beside it, and dishes of boiled and roasted meat, as many as there was room for, and a great glass of red wine gleamed so that it gladdened the heart.
sentences.n: “Tischchen, deck dich” = “Table, set yourself” — the wishing-table's command word. deck is the imperative of decken (“den Tisch decken” = to lay the table) with the reflexive dich. Say it, and the table lays itself; this magic phrase is the tale's own title and recurs throughout. rothem = 1857 spelling of rotem (red). “einem das Herz lachte” — the dative einem = “one's” (heart).

sentences.t: Der junge Gesell dachte „damit hast du genug für dein Lebtag,“ zog guter Dinge in der Welt umher und bekümmerte sich gar nicht darum ob ein Wirthshaus gut oder schlecht und ob etwas darin zu finden war, oder nicht.
sentences.l: The young journeyman thought “with that you have enough for your life-day,” drew of good cheer about in the world and troubled himself not at all about whether an inn was good or bad and whether anything was to be found in it, or not.
sentences.i: The young journeyman thought, “With this you have enough for the rest of your days,” and wandered the world in good spirits, not caring in the least whether an inn was good or bad, or whether there was anything to be had there or not.
sentences.n: der Gesell = a journeyman, a tradesman past apprenticeship. “für dein Lebtag” = for (all) your days. “guter Dinge” = in good spirits (a genitive idiom). “sich bekümmern um” = to worry about. Wirthshaus = inn (1857 th-, as in Wirth). The inn will be his undoing.

sentences.t: Wenn es ihm gefiel, so kehrte er gar nicht ein, sondern im Felde, im Wald, auf einer Wiese, wo er Lust hatte, nahm er sein Tischchen vom Rücken, stellte es vor sich und sprach „deck dich,“ so war alles da, was sein Herz begehrte.
sentences.l: When it pleased him, then he did not put in at all, but in the field, in the wood, on a meadow, wherever he had a mind, he took his little table from his back, set it before himself and spoke “cover yourself,” then all was there that his heart desired.
sentences.i: When he felt like it, he wouldn't stop at an inn at all, but in a field, in the woods, on a meadow, wherever he pleased, he took his little table off his back, set it in front of him and said “set yourself,” and there was everything his heart desired.
sentences.n: “einkehren” (to stop/put up at an inn) is separable: “kehrte… ein.” “wo er Lust hatte” = wherever he had the inclination. begehren = to desire, crave. Note the short command “deck dich” alone works too.

sentences.t: Endlich kam es ihm in den Sinn, er wollte zu seinem Vater zurückkehren, sein Zorn würde sich gelegt haben, und mit dem Tischchen deck dich würde er ihn gerne wieder aufnehmen.
sentences.l: At last it came into his mind, he wanted to return to his father, his wrath would have laid itself, and with the little-table-cover-yourself he would gladly take him in again.
sentences.i: At last it came into his mind that he wanted to go back to his father — his anger would have died down by now, and with the wishing-table the father would gladly take him in again.
sentences.n: “in den Sinn kommen” = to come to mind. “sich legen” of anger = to subside, die down; “würde… gelegt haben” is a conditional-perfect (would have subsided). Note “das Tischchen deck dich” has become the object's very name.

sentences.t: Es trug sich zu, daß er auf dem Heimweg Abends in ein Wirthshaus kam, das mit Gästen angefüllt war: sie hießen ihn willkommen und luden ihn ein sich zu ihnen zu setzen und mit ihnen zu essen, sonst würde er schwerlich noch etwas bekommen.
sentences.l: It came to pass that on the way home in the evening he came into an inn that was filled up with guests: they bade him welcome and invited him to sit himself with them and to eat with them, otherwise he would hardly get anything more.
sentences.i: It happened that on the way home one evening he came to an inn crowded with guests; they bade him welcome and invited him to sit with them and eat, or else he would scarcely get anything at all.
sentences.n: “es trug sich zu” = it came to pass (from sich zutragen) — an old narrative formula. “willkommen heißen” = to bid welcome. einladen (to invite) is separable, past luden… ein. schwerlich = hardly, scarcely.

sentences.t: „Nein,“ antwortete der Schreiner, „die paar Bissen will ich euch nicht vor dem Munde nehmen, lieber sollt ihr meine Gäste sein.“
sentences.l: “No,” answered the joiner, “the few morsels I will not take from before your mouth, rather you shall be my guests.”
sentences.i: “No,” answered the joiner, “I won't take the few bites out of your mouths — rather, you shall be my guests.”
sentences.n: “einem etwas vor dem Munde wegnehmen” = to take food out of someone's mouth. lieber = rather; sollt = the plain ihr-form (modern sollt/sollet).

sentences.t: Sie lachten und meinten er triebe seinen Spaß mit ihnen.
sentences.l: They laughed and thought he were driving his joke with them.
sentences.i: They laughed and thought he was making sport of them.
sentences.n: “seinen Spaß mit jemandem treiben” = to have one's fun with, poke fun at someone. triebe is subjunctive (reported thought): they supposed he was joking.

sentences.t: Er aber stellte sein hölzernes Tischchen mitten in die Stube und sprach „Tischchen, deck dich.“
sentences.l: But he set his wooden little table in the middle of the room and spoke “little-table, cover yourself.”
sentences.i: But he set his wooden little table in the middle of the room and said: “Table, set yourself.”
sentences.n: die Stube = the (common) room, parlour. Here the command is put to the test in public.

sentences.t: Augenblicklich war es mit Speisen besetzt, so gut wie sie der Wirth nicht hätte herbeischaffen können, und wovon der Geruch den Gästen lieblich in die Nase stieg.
sentences.l: Instantly it was set with dishes, as good as the innkeeper could not have brought forth, and whereof the smell rose lovelily to the guests' noses.
sentences.i: Instantly it was laden with food, better than the innkeeper could have provided, the smell of which rose sweetly to the guests' noses.
sentences.n: augenblicklich = in an instant. der Wirth = the innkeeper, host (1857 th-) — mark him, for he is the thief of this tale. “hätte… können” is subjunctive (could have). wovon = of which.

sentences.t: „Zugegriffen, liebe Freunde,“ sprach der Schreiner, und die Gäste, als sie sahen wie es gemeint war, ließen sich nicht zweimal bitten, rückten heran, zogen ihre Messer und griffen tapfer zu.
sentences.l: “Dig in, dear friends,” spoke the joiner, and the guests, when they saw how it was meant, did not let themselves be asked twice, moved up close, drew their knives and reached in bravely.
sentences.i: “Help yourselves, dear friends,” said the joiner, and the guests, when they saw how things stood, didn't need to be asked twice: they moved up, drew their knives, and fell to with a will.
sentences.n: “zugreifen” = to help oneself, dig in (separable; here the past participle as an invitation, and again as “griffen… zu”). “sich nicht zweimal bitten lassen” = not to need asking twice. “wie es gemeint war” = how it was meant/intended.

sentences.t: Und was sie am meisten verwunderte, wenn eine Schüssel leer geworden war, so stellte sich gleich von selbst eine volle an ihren Platz.
sentences.l: And what astonished them most, when a dish had become empty, then a full one placed itself at once by itself in its place.
sentences.i: And what astonished them most: whenever a dish had been emptied, a full one at once set itself in its place of its own accord.
sentences.n: “am meisten” = most (of all). “von selbst” = of its own accord, by itself. “sich stellen” here = to place itself.

sentences.t: Der Wirth stand in einer Ecke und sah dem Dinge zu; er wußte gar nicht was er sagen sollte, dachte aber „einen solchen Koch könntest du in deiner Wirthschaft wohl brauchen.“
sentences.l: The innkeeper stood in a corner and looked on at the thing; he knew not at all what he should say, but thought “such a cook you could well use in your inn.”
sentences.i: The innkeeper stood in a corner and watched; he had no idea what to say, but thought: “A cook like that would come in handy in my inn.”
sentences.n: “zusehen” (to look on, watch) is separable, taking a dative: “sah dem Dinge zu.” Wirthschaft = his inn/business (1857 th-). könntest… brauchen = could use; the seed of his theft is sown.

sentences.t: Der Schreiner und seine Gesellschaft waren lustig bis in die späte Nacht, endlich legten sie sich schlafen, und der junge Geselle gieng auch zu Bett und stellte sein Wünschtischchen an die Wand.
sentences.l: The joiner and his company were merry until the late night, finally they laid themselves to sleep, and the young journeyman went too to bed and set his wishing-table against the wall.
sentences.i: The joiner and his company were merry into the small hours; at last they lay down to sleep, and the young journeyman went to bed too and stood his wishing-table against the wall.
sentences.n: die Gesellschaft = the company. Wünschtischchen = wishing-table — the narrator's name for the gift; note it left carelessly by the wall.

sentences.t: Dem Wirthe aber ließen seine Gedanken keine Ruhe, es fiel ihm ein daß in seiner Rumpelkammer ein altes Tischchen stände, das gerade so aussähe: das holte er ganz sachte herbei und vertauschte es mit dem Wünschtischchen.
sentences.l: But the innkeeper's thoughts let him no rest, it occurred to him that in his junk-room there stood an old little table that looked just the same: that he fetched over quite softly and exchanged it with the wishing-table.
sentences.i: But the innkeeper's thoughts gave him no peace; it occurred to him that in his lumber-room stood an old little table that looked exactly the same. That one he fetched very softly and swapped it for the wishing-table.
sentences.n: The theft — the pattern the tale will repeat with all three brothers. “einfallen” (to occur to one) is separable: “fiel ihm ein.” stände and aussähe are subjunctives (there stood… that looked…, as he recalled). Wirthe keeps an old dative -e. vertauschen = to swap, exchange.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen zahlte der Schreiner sein Schlafgeld, packte sein Tischchen auf, dachte gar nicht daran daß er ein falsches hätte und gieng seiner Wege.
sentences.l: On the next morning the joiner paid his lodging-money, packed his little table up, thought not at all of it that he had a false one, and went his ways.
sentences.i: The next morning the joiner paid for his bed, packed up his table, never dreaming he had a fake one, and went on his way.
sentences.n: Schlafgeld = money paid for a night's lodging. “aufpacken” (to load up) is separable: “packte… auf.” “ein falsches” = a false/counterfeit one; hätte is subjunctive. “seiner Wege gehen” = to go on one's way (genitive idiom).

sentences.t: Zu Mittag kam er bei seinem Vater an, der ihn mit großer Freude empfieng.
sentences.l: At midday he arrived at his father's, who received him with great joy.
sentences.i: At noon he reached his father's house, and the father received him with great joy.
sentences.n: “ankommen” (to arrive) is separable: “kam… an.” empfieng is the 1857 spelling of empfing (received).

sentences.t: „Nun, mein lieber Sohn, was hast du gelernt?“ sagte er zu ihm.
sentences.l: “Now, my dear son, what have you learned?” said he to him.
sentences.i: “Well, my dear son, what have you learned?” he said to him.

sentences.t: „Vater, ich bin ein Schreiner geworden.“
sentences.l: “Father, I have become a joiner.”
sentences.n: geworden = become (past part. of werden). German uses sein (“ich bin… geworden”) as the auxiliary here, not haben.

sentences.t: „Ein gutes Handwerk,“ erwiederte der Alte, „aber was hast du von deiner Wanderschaft mitgebracht?“
sentences.l: “A good craft,” replied the old man, “but what have you brought back from your journeying?”
sentences.i: “A good trade,” replied the old man, “but what have you brought back from your travels?”
sentences.n: erwiederte is the 1857 spelling of erwiderte (replied). die Wanderschaft = a journeyman's travels. “mitbringen” (to bring along/back) is separable.

sentences.t: „Vater, das beste, was ich mitgebracht habe, ist das Tischchen.“
sentences.l: “Father, the best that I have brought back is the little table.”
sentences.i: “Father, the best thing I've brought back is the little table.”

sentences.t: Der Schneider betrachtete es von allen Seiten und sagte „daran hast du kein Meisterstück gemacht, das ist ein altes und schlechtes Tischchen.“
sentences.l: The tailor looked at it from all sides and said “on that you have made no masterpiece, that is an old and poor little table.”
sentences.i: The tailor examined it from every side and said: “You made no masterpiece there — it's an old, shabby little table.”
sentences.n: betrachten = to look closely at, examine. das Meisterstück = a masterpiece (the piece by which a journeyman qualifies as master); the father's jibe cuts at his trade.

sentences.t: „Aber es ist ein Tischchen deck dich,“ antwortete der Sohn, „wenn ich es hinstelle, und sage ihm es sollte sich decken, so stehen gleich die schönsten Gerichte darauf und ein Wein dabei, der das Herz erfreut.
sentences.l: “But it is a little-table-cover-yourself,” answered the son, “when I set it down, and tell it it should cover itself, then at once the finest dishes stand upon it and a wine beside it, that gladdens the heart.
sentences.i: “But it's a wishing-table,” answered the son; “when I set it down and tell it to set itself, at once the finest dishes stand on it, and a wine beside them to gladden the heart.
sentences.n: “hinstellen” (to set down) is separable. “es sollte sich decken” — subjunctive of the command in reported form. die Gerichte = dishes, courses; erfreuen = to gladden.

sentences.t: Ladet nur alle Verwandte und Freunde ein, die sollen sich einmal laben und erquicken, denn das Tischchen macht sie alle satt.“
sentences.l: Only invite all relatives and friends, they shall for once refresh and revive themselves, for the little table makes them all full.”
sentences.i: Just invite all our kin and friends — let them feast and refresh themselves for once, for the little table will fill them all.”
sentences.n: einladen (to invite) is separable, imperative “ladet… ein.” “sich laben” / “sich erquicken” = to refresh, regale oneself. His confidence is about to collapse.

sentences.t: Als die Gesellschaft beisammen war, stellte er sein Tischchen mitten in die Stube und sprach „Tischchen, deck dich.“
sentences.l: When the company was together, he set his little table in the middle of the room and spoke “little-table, cover yourself.”
sentences.i: When the company was assembled, he set his little table in the middle of the room and said: “Table, set yourself.”
sentences.n: beisammen = together, gathered.

sentences.t: Aber das Tischchen regte sich nicht und blieb so leer wie ein anderer Tisch, der die Sprache nicht versteht.
sentences.l: But the little table stirred not and stayed as empty as another table that does not understand the speech.
sentences.i: But the little table did not stir and stayed as bare as any other table that understands no language.
sentences.n: “sich regen” = to stir, move (reflexive). die Sprache = speech, language — a dry touch: an ordinary table “understands no speech.”

sentences.t: Da merkte der arme Geselle daß ihm das Tischchen vertauscht war, und schämte sich daß er wie ein Lügner da stand.
sentences.l: Then the poor journeyman noticed that the little table had been swapped on him, and was ashamed that he stood there like a liar.
sentences.i: Then the poor journeyman realized his table had been switched, and was ashamed to stand there like a liar.
sentences.n: “ihm… vertauscht war” — the dative ihm = “on him, to his loss.” “sich schämen” = to be ashamed (reflexive). He now shares his brothers' coming fate.

sentences.t: Die Verwandten aber lachten ihn aus, und mußten ungetrunken und ungegessen wieder heim wandern.
sentences.l: But the relatives laughed him out, and had to walk home again undrunk and uneaten.
sentences.i: But the relatives laughed him to scorn and had to trudge home again with nothing drunk or eaten.
sentences.n: “auslachen” (to laugh at, deride) is separable: “lachten ihn aus.” ungetrunken / ungegessen = without having drunk / eaten — coinages describing them, not the food.

sentences.t: Der Vater holte seine Lappen wieder herbei und schneiderte fort, der Sohn aber gieng bei einem Meister in die Arbeit.
sentences.l: The father fetched his rags out again and tailored on, but the son went into work with a master.
sentences.i: The father fetched out his cloth-scraps again and went back to his tailoring, while the son took work with a master.
sentences.n: die Lappen = rags, scraps of cloth (a tailor's offcuts). schneidern = to do tailoring; “fortschneidern” = to go on tailoring (separable, fort = onward).

sentences.t: Der zweite Sohn war zu einem Müller gekommen und bei ihm in die Lehre gegangen.
sentences.l: The second son had come to a miller and gone into apprenticeship with him.
sentences.i: The second son had come to a miller and taken his apprenticeship with him.

sentences.t: Als er seine Jahre herum hatte, sprach der Meister „weil du dich so wohl gehalten hast, so schenke ich dir einen Esel von einer besondern Art, er zieht nicht am Wagen und trägt auch keine Säcke.“
sentences.l: When he had his years up, the master spoke “because you have held yourself so well, I give you an ass of a special kind, he does not pull at the cart and carries no sacks either.”
sentences.i: When his years were up, the master said: “Because you've conducted yourself so well, I'll give you an ass of a special sort — he doesn't pull a cart and carries no sacks.”
sentences.n: “seine Jahre herum haben” = to have one's (apprentice) years finished. “sich wohl halten” = to conduct oneself well. schenken = to give as a gift. This ass is the second magic gift.

sentences.t: „Wozu ist er denn nütze?“ fragte der junge Geselle.
sentences.l: “For what is he then of use?” asked the young journeyman.
sentences.i: “What's he good for, then?” asked the young journeyman.
sentences.n: “wozu nütze sein” = to be of use for what. denn in a question = “then, anyway” — softening, colloquial.

sentences.t: „Er speit Gold,“ antwortete der Müller, „wenn du ihn auf ein Tuch stellst und sprichst „Bricklebrit,“ so speit dir das gute Thier Goldstücke aus, hinten und vorn.“
sentences.l: “He spits gold,” answered the miller, “when you set him on a cloth and speak ‘Bricklebrit,’ then the good beast spits out gold pieces for you, behind and in front.”
sentences.i: “He spits gold,” answered the miller; “when you set him on a cloth and say ‘Bricklebrit,’ the good beast spews out gold coins for you, front and back.”
sentences.n: “Bricklebrit” is the gold-ass's command word — a nonsense syllable with no meaning, like an abracadabra; say it and the ass produces gold. speien = to spit, spew; “ausspeien” is separable (“speit… aus”). das Tuch = a cloth (to catch the coins).

sentences.t: „Das ist eine schöne Sache,“ sprach der Geselle, dankte dem Meister und zog in die Welt.
sentences.l: “That is a fine thing,” spoke the journeyman, thanked the master and drew into the world.
sentences.i: “That's a fine thing,” said the journeyman, thanked the master and set off into the world.
sentences.n: “in die Welt ziehen” = to go out into the world (ziehen of travel = to journey, move).

sentences.t: Wenn er Gold nöthig hatte, brauchte er nur zu seinem Esel „Bricklebrit“ zu sagen, so regnete es Goldstücke, und er hatte weiter keine Mühe als sie von der Erde aufzuheben.
sentences.l: When he had gold needful, he needed only to say ‘Bricklebrit’ to his ass, then it rained gold pieces, and he had no further trouble than to pick them up from the ground.
sentences.i: Whenever he needed gold, he had only to say ‘Bricklebrit’ to his ass, and it rained gold coins; his only trouble was picking them up off the ground.
sentences.n: nöthig = 1857 spelling of nötig (necessary). “brauchen nur zu” = to need only to. “so regnete es Goldstücke” — impersonal es with a rain of gold. aufheben (to pick up) is separable (zu-infinitive aufzuheben).

sentences.t: Wo er hinkam, war ihm das beste gut genug, und je theurer je lieber, denn er hatte immer einen vollen Beutel.
sentences.l: Wherever he came, the best was good enough for him, and the dearer the better, for he always had a full purse.
sentences.i: Wherever he went, the best was just good enough for him — the dearer the better — for his purse was always full.
sentences.n: “je theurer je lieber” = the dearer the more welcome (an older “je… je…” where modern German says “je… desto…”). theurer = 1857 spelling of teurer. der Beutel = purse, pouch.

sentences.t: Als er sich eine Zeit lang in der Welt umgesehen hatte, dachte er „du mußt deinen Vater aufsuchen, wenn du mit dem Goldesel kommst, so wird er seinen Zorn vergessen und dich gut aufnehmen.“
sentences.l: When he had looked himself around in the world for a while, he thought “you must seek out your father; when you come with the gold-ass, then he will forget his wrath and take you in well.”
sentences.i: When he had looked about the world for a while, he thought: “You must go and find your father; if you come with the gold-ass, he'll forget his anger and take you in gladly.”
sentences.n: “sich umsehen” = to look about (reflexive, separable). aufsuchen = to seek out, go to find. der Goldesel = the gold-ass — the tale's second title-object. “aufnehmen” = to take in, receive.

sentences.t: Es trug sich zu, daß er in dasselbe Wirthshaus gerieth, in welchem seinem Bruder das Tischchen vertauscht war.
sentences.l: It came to pass that he got into the same inn in which his brother's little table had been swapped.
sentences.i: It happened that he came upon the very same inn where his brother's table had been switched.
sentences.n: “es trug sich zu” = it came to pass (as with the first son). gerieth (1857 for geriet) = ended up, chanced. The trap is the same inn, the same thieving host.

sentences.t: Er führte seinen Esel an der Hand, und der Wirth wollte ihm das Thier abnehmen und anbinden, der junge Geselle aber sprach „gebt euch keine Mühe, meinen Grauschimmel führe ich selbst in den Stall und binde ihn auch selbst an, denn ich muß wissen wo er steht.“
sentences.l: He led his ass by the hand, and the innkeeper wanted to take the beast from him and tie it up, but the young journeyman spoke “give yourselves no trouble, my grey-nag I lead into the stall myself and tie him up myself too, for I must know where he stands.”
sentences.i: He led his ass by the hand, and the innkeeper wanted to take the beast and stable it, but the young journeyman said: “Don't trouble yourself — I'll lead my grey into the stall myself and tie him up myself, for I must know where he stands.”
sentences.n: “einem etwas abnehmen” = to take something off someone's hands. anbinden (to tie up) is separable: “binde… an.” Grauschimmel = a grey horse — he calls the ass this out of pride. gebt = polite plural imperative to the host.

sentences.t: Dem Wirth kam das wunderlich vor, und er meinte einer, der seinen Esel selbst besorgen müßte, hätte nicht viel zu verzehren: als aber der Fremde in die Tasche griff, zwei Goldstücke heraus holte und sagte er sollte nur etwas gutes für ihn einkaufen, so machte er große Augen, lief und suchte das beste, das er auftreiben konnte.
sentences.l: To the innkeeper that seemed strange, and he thought one who had to tend his ass himself would not have much to consume: but when the stranger reached into his pocket, drew out two gold pieces and said he should just buy something good for him, then he made big eyes, ran and sought the best that he could rustle up.
sentences.i: The innkeeper found that odd, and thought a man who had to see to his own ass would not have much to spend; but when the stranger reached into his pocket, took out two gold pieces and told him to buy something good for himself, he made big eyes, and ran to find the best he could get.
sentences.n: “wunderlich vorkommen” = to strike one as strange (separable: “kam… vor”); müßte, hätte are subjunctive (reported supposition). “große Augen machen” = to stare, be amazed. verzehren = to consume, spend on food; auftreiben = to rustle up, procure.

sentences.t: Nach der Mahlzeit fragte der Gast was er schuldig wäre, der Wirth wollte die doppelte Kreide nicht sparen und sagte noch ein paar Goldstücke müßte er zulegen.
sentences.l: After the meal the guest asked what he was owing, the innkeeper would not spare the double chalk and said he must add on yet a couple of gold pieces.
sentences.i: After the meal the guest asked what he owed; the innkeeper was not sparing with double the reckoning, and said he'd have to add a few more gold pieces.
sentences.n: schuldig = owing, in debt; wäre and müßte are subjunctive (reported). “doppelte Kreide” — innkeepers chalked up the tab, so to “not spare the double chalk” = to charge double. zulegen = to add on.

sentences.t: Der Geselle griff in die Tasche, aber sein Gold war eben zu Ende.
sentences.l: The journeyman reached into his pocket, but his gold was just at an end.
sentences.i: The journeyman reached into his pocket, but his gold had just run out.
sentences.n: eben here = just (at that moment). “zu Ende sein” = to be finished, run out.

sentences.t: „Wartet einen Augenblick, Herr Wirth,“ sprach er, „ich will nur gehen und Gold holen;“ nahm aber das Tischtuch mit.
sentences.l: “Wait a moment, Mr. Innkeeper,” spoke he, “I will just go and fetch gold;” but took the tablecloth along.
sentences.i: “Wait a moment, landlord,” he said, “I'll just go and fetch some gold” — but he took the tablecloth along with him.
sentences.n: Wartet = polite plural imperative (wait!). das Tischtuch = tablecloth — he needs it under the ass to catch the coins. “mitnehmen” (to take along) is separable: “nahm… mit.”

sentences.t: Der Wirth wußte nicht was das heißen sollte, war neugierig, schlich ihm nach, und da der Gast die Stallthüre zuriegelte, so guckte er durch ein Astloch.
sentences.l: The innkeeper knew not what that should mean, was curious, crept after him, and since the guest bolted the stable door, he peeped through a knothole.
sentences.i: The innkeeper had no idea what this was about, grew curious, crept after him, and since the guest bolted the stable door, he peeped through a knothole.
sentences.n: “was das heißen sollte” = what that was supposed to mean. “nachschleichen” (to creep after) is separable, taking a dative: “schlich ihm nach.” Stallthüre = stable door (1857 th-). zuriegeln = to bolt; das Astloch = a knothole.

sentences.t: Der Fremde breitete unter dem Esel das Tuch aus, rief „Bricklebrit,“ und augenblicklich fieng das Thier an Gold zu speien von hinten und vorn, daß es ordentlich auf die Erde herabregnete.
sentences.l: The stranger spread out the cloth under the ass, cried ‘Bricklebrit,’ and instantly the beast began to spit gold behind and in front, so that it downright rained down onto the ground.
sentences.i: The stranger spread the cloth under the ass, called ‘Bricklebrit,’ and instantly the beast began to spit gold, front and back, so that it fairly rained down onto the ground.
sentences.n: “ausbreiten” (to spread out) is separable: “breitete… aus.” anfangen (to begin) is separable, 1857 spelling fieng an. ordentlich here = downright, properly; herabregnen = to rain down.

sentences.t: „Ei der tausend,“ sagte der Wirth, „da sind die Ducaten bald geprägt! so ein Geldbeutel ist nicht übel!“
sentences.l: “My, the thousand,” said the innkeeper, “there the ducats are soon minted! such a money-bag is not bad!”
sentences.i: “Well I never!” said the innkeeper. “There the ducats are minted in no time! A money-bag like that is not to be sniffed at!”
sentences.n: “ei der tausend” is an old exclamation of astonishment (“good gracious!”). Ducaten = ducats (gold coins); prägen = to mint, stamp. “nicht übel” = not bad — a dry understatement as he plots the theft.

sentences.t: Der Gast bezahlte seine Zeche und legte sich schlafen, der Wirth aber schlich in der Nacht herab in den Stall, führte den Münzmeister weg und band einen andern Esel an seine Stelle.
sentences.l: The guest paid his bill and laid himself to sleep, but the innkeeper crept down into the stall in the night, led the mint-master away and tied another ass in his place.
sentences.i: The guest paid his reckoning and lay down to sleep, but in the night the innkeeper crept down to the stall, led the mint-master away and tied another ass in its place.
sentences.n: die Zeche = the bill, tab at an inn. Münzmeister = mint-master, a joking epithet for the gold-ass. “wegführen” (to lead away) is separable: “führte… weg.” The theft is done, exactly as with the table.

sentences.t: Den folgenden Morgen in der Frühe zog der Geselle mit seinem Esel ab und meinte er hätte seinen Goldesel.
sentences.l: The following morning in the early hour the journeyman drew off with his ass and thought he had his gold-ass.
sentences.i: Early the next morning the journeyman set off with his ass, believing he had his gold-ass.
sentences.n: “in der Frühe” = early in the morning. “abziehen” (to move off, depart) is separable: “zog… ab.” hätte is subjunctive (he supposed).

sentences.t: Mittags kam er bei seinem Vater an, der sich freute als er ihn wiedersah und ihn gerne aufnahm.
sentences.l: At midday he arrived at his father's, who rejoiced when he saw him again and gladly took him in.
sentences.i: At midday he reached his father's house; the father was glad to see him again and gladly took him in.
sentences.n: Mittags = at midday (adverbial genitive). “wiedersehen” = to see again; “aufnehmen” = to take in — both separable.

sentences.t: „Was ist aus dir geworden, mein Sohn?“ fragte der Alte.
sentences.l: “What has become of you, my son?” asked the old man.
sentences.i: “What has become of you, my son?” asked the old man.
sentences.n: “aus jemandem werden” = to become of someone (what has he made of himself).

sentences.t: „Ein Müller, lieber Vater,“ antwortete er.
sentences.l: “A miller, dear father,” answered he.
sentences.i: “A miller, dear father,” he answered.

sentences.t: „Was hast du von deiner Wanderschaft mitgebracht?“
sentences.l: “What have you brought back from your journeying?”
sentences.i: “What have you brought back from your travels?”

sentences.t: „Weiter nichts als einen Esel.“
sentences.l: “Nothing further than an ass.”
sentences.i: “Nothing but an ass.”
sentences.n: “weiter nichts als” = nothing more than, nothing but.

sentences.t: „Esel gibts hier genug,“ sagte der Vater, „da wäre mir doch eine gute Ziege lieber gewesen.“
sentences.l: “Asses there are enough here,” said the father, “a good goat would have been dearer to me than that.”
sentences.i: “There are asses enough here,” said the father; “I'd sooner have had a good goat.”
sentences.n: gibts = gibt es (there is/are), contracted. “mir wäre… lieber gewesen” = I would have preferred (dative + comparative + subjunctive). Note the irony — the goat is what ruined him.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ antwortete der Sohn, „aber es ist kein gemeiner Esel, sondern ein Goldesel: wenn ich sage „Bricklebrit,“ so speit euch das gute Thier ein ganzes Tuch voll Goldstücke.
sentences.l: “Yes,” answered the son, “but it is no common ass, but a gold-ass: when I say ‘Bricklebrit,’ then the good beast spits you a whole cloth full of gold pieces.
sentences.i: “Yes,” answered the son, “but it's no common ass — it's a gold-ass: when I say ‘Bricklebrit,’ the good beast spits you a whole clothful of gold coins.
sentences.n: gemein here = common, ordinary (not “mean”). “ein ganzes Tuch voll” = a whole clothful.

sentences.t: Laßt nur alle Verwandte herbei rufen, ich mache sie alle zu reichen Leuten.“
sentences.l: Only have all relatives called hither, I will make them all into rich people.”
sentences.i: Just send for all our kin — I'll make them all rich.”
sentences.n: Laßt = plural imperative of lassen (have/let). “zu reichen Leuten machen” = to make into rich folk. His boast walks into the same fall as his brother's.

sentences.t: „Das laß ich mir gefallen,“ sagte der Schneider, „dann brauch ich mich mit der Nadel nicht weiter zu quälen,“ sprang selbst fort, und rief die Verwandten herbei.
sentences.l: “That I let please me,” said the tailor, “then I need not torment myself further with the needle,” sprang off himself, and called the relatives hither.
sentences.i: “That I like the sound of,” said the tailor, “then I needn't wear myself out with the needle any longer,” and he leapt off himself and summoned the relatives.
sentences.n: “sich etwas gefallen lassen” = to be happy with, put up with something (here: “that suits me fine”). “sich quälen” = to torment oneself, toil. brauch = brauche (1st sg.), clipped.

sentences.t: Sobald sie beisammen waren, hieß sie der Müller Platz machen, breitete sein Tuch aus, und brachte den Esel in die Stube.
sentences.l: As soon as they were together, the miller bade them make room, spread out his cloth, and brought the ass into the room.
sentences.i: As soon as they were assembled, the miller bade them make room, spread out his cloth, and brought the ass into the parlour.
sentences.n: sobald = as soon as. “heißen” + infinitive = to bid/tell someone to do (“hieß sie Platz machen” = bade them make room).

sentences.t: „Jetzt gebt acht“ sagte er und rief „Bricklebrit,“ aber es waren keine Goldstücke was herabfiel, und es zeigte sich, daß das Thier nichts von der Kunst verstand, denn es bringts nicht jeder Esel so weit.
sentences.l: “Now pay heed” said he and cried ‘Bricklebrit,’ but it was no gold pieces that fell down, and it showed itself that the beast understood nothing of the art, for not every ass brings it so far.
sentences.i: “Now watch closely,” he said, and called ‘Bricklebrit’ — but it was no gold coins that fell, and it turned out the beast knew nothing of the art, for not every ass gets that far.
sentences.n: “acht geben” = to pay attention (gebt acht = pay heed!). “sich zeigen” = to become apparent. bringts = bringt es; “es so weit bringen” = to get that far, achieve as much — the narrator's wry proverb.

sentences.t: Da machte der arme Müller ein langes Gesicht, sah daß er betrogen war und bat die Verwandten um Verzeihung, die so arm heim giengen, als sie gekommen waren.
sentences.l: Then the poor miller made a long face, saw that he had been deceived and begged the relatives for pardon, who went home as poor as they had come.
sentences.i: Then the poor miller pulled a long face, saw he'd been cheated, and begged the relatives' pardon; they went home as poor as they had come.
sentences.n: “ein langes Gesicht machen” = to pull a long face. “um Verzeihung bitten” = to beg pardon. giengen = 1857 spelling of gingen (went).

sentences.t: Es blieb nichts übrig, der Alte mußte wieder nach der Nadel greifen, und der Junge sich bei einem Müller verdingen.
sentences.l: There remained nothing over, the old man had to reach again for the needle, and the young one to hire himself out with a miller.
sentences.i: Nothing else for it: the old man had to take up the needle again, and the young one had to hire himself out to a miller.
sentences.n: “es bleibt nichts übrig” = there is nothing else for it. “nach etwas greifen” = to reach for. “sich verdingen” = to hire oneself out (reflexive) — the verb is understood again in the second clause.

sentences.t: Der dritte Bruder war zu einem Drechsler in die Lehre gegangen, und weil es ein kunstreiches Handwerk ist, mußte er am längsten lernen.
sentences.l: The third brother had gone into apprenticeship with a turner, and because it is a skill-rich craft, he had to learn the longest.
sentences.i: The third brother had apprenticed himself to a turner, and because it is a craft requiring great skill, he had to learn the longest.
sentences.n: der Drechsler = a turner (one who shapes wood on a lathe). kunstreich = requiring much art/skill. “am längsten” = the longest.

sentences.t: Seine Brüder aber meldeten ihm in einem Briefe wie schlimm es ihnen ergangen wäre, und wie sie der Wirth noch am letzten Abende um ihre schönen Wünschdinge gebracht hätte.
sentences.l: But his brothers reported to him in a letter how badly it had gone for them, and how the innkeeper had, on the very last evening, brought them out of their fine wishing-things.
sentences.i: But his brothers wrote to him how badly they had fared, and how the innkeeper, on the very last evening, had cheated them of their fine magic gifts.
sentences.n: “jemandem um etwas bringen” = to cheat someone out of something. ergangen wäre and gebracht hätte are subjunctive — reported speech (what the letter said). Briefe and Abende keep old dative -e endings. Wünschdinge = wishing-things, the magic gifts; the third son is now forewarned.

sentences.t: Als der Drechsler nun ausgelernt hatte und wandern sollte, so schenkte ihm sein Meister, weil er sich so wohl gehalten, einen Sack, und sagte „es liegt ein Knüppel darin.“
sentences.l: When the turner had now finished learning and was to go journeying, then his master gave him, because he had held himself so well, a sack, and said “there lies a cudgel in it.”
sentences.i: When the turner had finished his training and was to set out, his master, because he had conducted himself so well, gave him a sack and said: “There's a cudgel inside.”
sentences.n: auslernen = to finish one's apprenticeship. der Sack = sack, bag; der Knüppel = a cudgel, club — the third magic gift, and the one that turns the tables. darin = in it, therein.

sentences.t: „Den Sack kann ich umhängen, und er kann mir gute Dienste leisten, aber was soll der Knüppel darin? der macht ihn nur schwer.“
sentences.l: “The sack I can hang about me, and it can render me good services, but what use is the cudgel in it? it only makes it heavy.”
sentences.i: “The sack I can sling over my shoulder, and it can serve me well — but what's the cudgel in it for? It only makes it heavy.”
sentences.n: “umhängen” = to hang around oneself, sling over the shoulder. “Dienste leisten” = to render service. “was soll…?” = what is the point of…? der (“der macht ihn schwer”) = a demonstrative “it,” pointing back to the cudgel.

sentences.t: „Das will ich dir sagen,“ antwortete der Meister, „hat dir jemand etwas zu leid gethan, so sprich nur „Knüppel, aus dem Sack,“ so springt dir der Knüppel heraus unter die Leute und tanzt ihnen so lustig auf dem Rücken herum, daß sie sich acht Tage lang nicht regen und bewegen können; und eher läßt er nicht ab als bis du sagst: „Knüppel, in den Sack.“
sentences.l: “That I will tell you,” answered the master, “if someone has done you some harm, then just speak ‘Cudgel, out of the sack,’ then the cudgel springs out to you among the people and dances so merrily about on their backs, that they cannot stir and move for eight days long; and he leaves not off sooner than until you say: ‘Cudgel, into the sack.’
sentences.i: “That I'll tell you,” answered the master; “if anyone has done you harm, just say ‘Cudgel, out of the sack!’ and the cudgel leaps out among them and dances so merrily on their backs that they can't stir for eight days; and it won't stop until you say ‘Cudgel, into the sack.’
sentences.n: “Knüppel, aus dem Sack” and “Knüppel, in den Sack” are the cudgel's command words — one to loose it, one to call it home; unlike the other two gifts, this one has a matched pair of commands. gethan = 1857 spelling of getan (done); “zu leid thun” = to do harm/wrong. “abläßt” (to leave off, desist) is separable: “läßt… ab.”

sentences.t: Der Gesell dankte ihm, hieng den Sack um, und wenn ihm jemand zu nahe kam und auf den Leib wollte, so sprach er „Knüppel, aus dem Sack,“ alsbald sprang der Knüppel heraus und klopfte einem nach dem andern den Rock oder Wams gleich auf dem Rücken aus, und wartete nicht erst bis er ihn ausgezogen hatte; und das gieng so geschwind, daß eh sichs einer versah die Reihe schon an ihm war.
sentences.l: The journeyman thanked him, hung the sack about him, and when someone came too near to him and was after his body, then he spoke ‘Cudgel, out of the sack,’ at once the cudgel sprang out and beat, one after the other, the coat or jerkin clean out right on the back, and did not wait first until he had taken it off; and that went so swiftly, that before one knew it the turn was already on him.
sentences.i: The journeyman thanked him, slung on the sack, and whenever anyone came too close and meant him harm, he'd say ‘Cudgel, out of the sack,’ and at once the cudgel sprang out and dusted the coat or jerkin on their backs, one after another, without waiting for them to take it off; and it went so fast that before a man knew it, his turn had come.
sentences.n: “umhängen” separable, 1857 spelling hieng… um. “auf den Leib wollen” = to be out to attack someone. “den Rock ausklopfen” = to beat the dust from a coat — here of beating the man in it; “einem” = a dative “for one/somebody.” das Wams = a jerkin, doublet. “ehe sichs einer versah” = before one knew it (“sich versehen” = to be caught unawares).

sentences.t: Der junge Drechsler langte zur Abendzeit in dem Wirthshaus an, wo seine Brüder waren betrogen worden.
sentences.l: The young turner arrived at evening-time at the inn, where his brothers had been deceived.
sentences.i: The young turner reached the inn at evening, where his brothers had been cheated.
sentences.n: “anlangen” (to arrive) is separable: “langte… an.” “waren betrogen worden” is a past-perfect passive (had been deceived).

sentences.t: Er legte seinen Ranzen vor sich auf den Tisch und fieng an zu erzählen was er alles merkwürdiges in der Welt gesehen habe.
sentences.l: He laid his knapsack before himself on the table and began to tell what all remarkable things he had seen in the world.
sentences.i: He laid his knapsack on the table before him and began to tell of all the remarkable things he had seen in the world.
sentences.n: der Ranzen = knapsack, pack. anfangen (to begin) separable, 1857 fieng an. “was… alles merkwürdiges” = all the remarkable things; habe is subjunctive (reported).

sentences.t: „Ja,“ sagte er, „man findet wohl ein Tischchen deck dich, einen Goldesel und dergleichen: lauter gute Dinge, die ich nicht verachte, aber das ist alles nichts gegen den Schatz, den ich mir erworben habe und mit mir da in meinem Sack führe.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” said he, “one may well find a little-table-cover-yourself, a gold-ass and the like: nothing but good things, which I do not despise, but that is all nothing against the treasure that I have acquired and carry with me there in my sack.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” he said, “you may well find a wishing-table, a gold-ass and the like — all good things, which I don't despise — but that's all nothing next to the treasure I've won and carry here in my sack.”
sentences.n: The bait for the greedy host — deliberate. dergleichen = the like. lauter = nothing but. verachten = to despise. der Schatz = treasure; erwerben = to acquire, win.

sentences.t: Der Wirth spitzte die Ohren: „was in aller Welt mag das sein?“ dachte er „der Sack ist wohl mit lauter Edelsteinen angefüllt; den sollte ich billig auch noch haben, denn aller guten Dinge sind drei.“
sentences.l: The innkeeper pricked up his ears: “what in all the world may that be?” thought he “the sack is surely filled with nothing but jewels; that I should by rights have too, for of all good things there are three.”
sentences.i: The innkeeper pricked up his ears: “What in the world can that be?” he thought. “The sack must be crammed with jewels — I ought by rights to have that one too, for all good things come in threes.”
sentences.n: “die Ohren spitzen” = to prick up one's ears. angefüllt = filled up, crammed. billig here = by rights, fairly (not “cheap”). “aller guten Dinge sind drei” = “all good things come in threes,” a proverb — the host means this is the third theft he'll pull off. His greed sets the trap for himself.

sentences.t: Als Schlafenszeit war, streckte sich der Gast auf die Bank und legte seinen Sack als Kopfkissen unter.
sentences.l: When bedtime was, the guest stretched himself out on the bench and laid his sack underneath as a pillow.
sentences.i: When it was time to sleep, the guest stretched out on the bench and laid his sack under his head as a pillow.
sentences.n: Schlafenszeit = bedtime. “sich strecken” = to stretch out. “unterlegen” (to lay underneath) is separable: “legte… unter.” He is only pretending to sleep.

sentences.t: Der Wirth als er meinte der Gast läge in tiefem Schlaf, gieng herbei, rückte und zog ganz sachte und vorsichtig an dem Sack, ob er ihn vielleicht wegziehen und einen andern unterlegen könnte.
sentences.l: The innkeeper, when he thought the guest lay in deep sleep, went over, shifted and pulled quite softly and carefully at the sack, whether he might perhaps draw it away and lay another underneath.
sentences.i: The innkeeper, thinking the guest lay in a deep sleep, came over and tugged very softly and carefully at the sack, to see whether he might pull it away and slip another one under his head.
sentences.n: läge is subjunctive (he thought the guest lay…). sachte = softly, gently. wegziehen = to pull away, unterlegen = to put underneath; könnte is subjunctive (whether he could). The theft begins exactly as before — but this time the gift fights back.

sentences.t: Der Drechsler aber hatte schon lange darauf gewartet, wie nun der Wirth eben einen herzhaften Ruck thun wollte, rief er „Knüppel, aus dem Sack.“
sentences.l: But the turner had already long waited for it, and as the innkeeper was just about to give a hearty jerk, he cried ‘Cudgel, out of the sack.’
sentences.i: But the turner had long been waiting for just this, and as the innkeeper was about to give a good hard tug, he cried: ‘Cudgel, out of the sack!’
sentences.n: “darauf warten” = to wait for it. der Ruck = a jerk, tug; herzhaft = hearty, vigorous; thun = 1857 thun (to do/give). Now the trap the host walked into springs shut.

sentences.t: Alsbald fuhr das Knüppelchen heraus, dem Wirth auf den Leib, und rieb ihm die Nähte daß es eine Art hatte.
sentences.l: At once the little cudgel shot out, onto the innkeeper's body, and rubbed him the seams so that it was something.
sentences.i: At once the little cudgel shot out onto the innkeeper's back and worked him over so that it was a sight to see.
sentences.n: Knüppelchen is a diminutive of Knüppel — almost affectionate for so brutal a stick. “jemandem die Nähte reiben” = to work someone over, thrash him (“rub his seams,” a tailor's-shop image again). “daß es eine Art hatte” = so that it was really something, a proper job of it.

sentences.t: Der Wirth schrie zum Erbarmen, aber je lauter er schrie, desto kräftiger schlug der Knüppel ihm den Tact dazu auf dem Rücken, bis er endlich erschöpft zur Erde fiel.
sentences.l: The innkeeper screamed to pity, but the louder he screamed, the more powerfully the cudgel beat the time to it on his back, until at last he fell exhausted to the ground.
sentences.i: The innkeeper screamed fit to move the heart, but the louder he screamed, the harder the cudgel beat time on his back, until at last he collapsed exhausted to the floor.
sentences.n: “zum Erbarmen schreien” = to cry out pitifully. “je… desto…” = the… the… (modern German uses these two together). Tact = 1857 spelling of Takt (beat, time); “den Takt schlagen” = to beat time, here a grim joke. erschöpft = exhausted.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Drechsler „wo du das Tischchen deck dich und den Goldesel nicht wieder heraus gibst, so soll der Tanz von neuem angehen.“
sentences.l: Then the turner spoke “if you do not give the little-table-cover-yourself and the gold-ass out again, then the dance shall begin anew.”
sentences.i: Then the turner said: “If you don't hand back the wishing-table and the gold-ass, the dance shall start all over again.”
sentences.n: wo here = if (an older use of wo for wenn). “herausgeben” (to hand back) is separable: “gibst… heraus.” “von neuem” = anew, all over again; angehen = to begin, start up.

sentences.t: „Ach nein,“ rief der Wirth ganz kleinlaut, „ich gebe alles gerne wieder heraus, laßt nur den verwünschten Kobold wieder in den Sack kriechen.“
sentences.l: “Ah no,” cried the innkeeper quite meek, “I gladly give everything out again, only let the accursed goblin creep into the sack again.”
sentences.i: “Oh no,” cried the innkeeper, quite crestfallen, “I'll gladly hand it all back — only let that accursed goblin crawl into the sack again.”
sentences.n: kleinlaut = meek, subdued, crestfallen. verwünscht = accursed, confounded; der Kobold = a goblin, imp — what the host now calls the cudgel. kriechen = to crawl, creep.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Geselle „ich will Gnade für Recht ergehen lassen, aber hüte dich vor Schaden!“ dann rief er „Knüppel, in den Sack!“ und ließ ihn ruhen.
sentences.l: Then the journeyman spoke “I will let mercy go before justice, but guard yourself against harm!” then he cried ‘Cudgel, into the sack!’ and let it rest.
sentences.i: Then the journeyman said: “I'll temper justice with mercy — but beware of doing harm!” Then he cried ‘Cudgel, into the sack!’ and let it rest.
sentences.n: “Gnade für Recht ergehen lassen” = to show mercy instead of strict justice (a set phrase). “sich vor etwas hüten” = to beware of, guard against. The second command word sends the cudgel home.

sentences.t: Der Drechsler zog am andern Morgen mit dem Tischchen deck dich und dem Goldesel heim zu seinem Vater.
sentences.l: The turner drew home the next morning with the little-table-cover-yourself and the gold-ass to his father.
sentences.i: The next morning the turner set off home to his father with the wishing-table and the gold-ass.
sentences.n: “heimziehen” (to travel home) is separable: “zog… heim.”

sentences.t: Der Schneider freute sich als er ihn wieder sah, und fragte auch ihn was er in der Fremde gelernt hätte.
sentences.l: The tailor rejoiced when he saw him again, and asked him too what he had learned in foreign parts.
sentences.i: The tailor was glad to see him again, and asked him too what he had learned abroad.
sentences.n: “sich freuen” = to be glad (reflexive). “in der Fremde” = abroad, in foreign parts. hätte is subjunctive (reported question).

sentences.t: „Lieber Vater,“ antwortete er, „ich bin ein Drechsler geworden.“
sentences.l: “Dear father,” answered he, “I have become a turner.”
sentences.i: “Dear father,” he answered, “I've become a turner.”

sentences.t: „Ein kunstreiches Handwerk,“ sagte der Vater, „was hast du von der Wanderschaft mitgebracht?“
sentences.l: “A skill-rich craft,” said the father, “what have you brought back from your journeying?”
sentences.i: “A craft that takes real skill,” said the father. “What have you brought back from your travels?”

sentences.t: „Ein kostbares Stück, lieber Vater,“ antwortete der Sohn, „einen Knüppel in dem Sack.“
sentences.l: “A precious piece, dear father,” answered the son, “a cudgel in the sack.”
sentences.i: “A precious thing, dear father,” answered the son, “a cudgel in the sack.”
sentences.n: kostbar = precious, costly — a deliberate overstatement to provoke the father, who cannot see the point of a mere stick.

sentences.t: „Was!“ rief der Vater, „einen Knüppel! das ist der Mühe werth! den kannst du dir von jedem Baume abhauen.“
sentences.l: “What!” cried the father, “a cudgel! that is worth the trouble! that you can hack yourself from any tree.”
sentences.i: “What!” cried the father, “a cudgel! Well, that was worth the effort! You can cut yourself one from any tree.”
sentences.n: “der Mühe werth” = worth the trouble — said sarcastically. “abhauen” (to hack/cut off) is separable: “kannst… abhauen”; “dir” = a dative “for yourself.”

sentences.t: „Aber einen solchen nicht, lieber Vater: sage ich „Knüppel, aus dem Sack,“ so springt der Knüppel heraus und macht mit dem, der es nicht gut mit mir meint, einen schlimmen Tanz, und läßt nicht eher nach als bis er auf der Erde liegt und um gut Wetter bittet.
sentences.l: “But not such a one, dear father: if I say ‘Cudgel, out of the sack,’ then the cudgel springs out and makes with him who means me no good a bad dance, and does not leave off sooner than until he lies on the ground and begs for good weather.
sentences.i: “But not one like this, dear father: if I say ‘Cudgel, out of the sack,’ the cudgel leaps out and leads a nasty dance with anyone who wishes me ill, and won't leave off until he's flat on the ground begging for mercy.
sentences.n: “es gut mit jemandem meinen” = to mean someone well; here the negative. “nachlassen” (to let up, leave off) is separable: “läßt… nach.” “um gut Wetter bitten” = to beg for mercy, sue for peace — literally “to ask for good weather.”

sentences.t: Seht ihr, mit diesem Knüppel habe ich das Tischchen deck dich und den Goldesel wieder herbei geschafft, die der diebische Wirth meinen Brüdern abgenommen hatte.
sentences.l: See, with this cudgel I have brought back the little-table-cover-yourself and the gold-ass, which the thievish innkeeper had taken from my brothers.
sentences.i: You see, with this cudgel I've won back the wishing-table and the gold-ass that the thieving innkeeper took from my brothers.
sentences.n: “herbeischaffen” (to fetch here, get back) is separable, past part. herbei… geschafft. diebisch = thieving. “einem etwas abnehmen” = to take something from someone.

sentences.t: Jetzt laßt sie beide rufen und ladet alle Verwandten ein, ich will sie speisen und tränken und will ihnen die Taschen noch mit Gold füllen.“
sentences.l: Now have them both called and invite all relatives, I will feed and give them drink and will fill their pockets besides with gold.”
sentences.i: Now send for them both and invite all our kin — I'll feast them and give them drink, and fill their pockets with gold besides.”
