sentences.n: laßt… rufen = have (them) summoned. einladen (to invite) separable: “ladet… ein.” speisen = to feed, tränken = to give to drink (causative of trinken). Now, unlike his brothers, his boast will hold.

sentences.t: Der alte Schneider wollte nicht recht trauen, brachte aber doch die Verwandten zusammen.
sentences.l: The old tailor did not quite want to trust it, but brought the relatives together after all.
sentences.i: The old tailor could not quite believe it, but gathered the relatives all the same.
sentences.n: “nicht recht trauen” = to not quite trust, be doubtful. doch here = after all, all the same.

sentences.t: Da deckte der Drechsler ein Tuch in die Stube, führte den Goldesel herein und sagte zu seinem Bruder „nun, lieber Bruder, sprich mit ihm.“
sentences.l: Then the turner spread a cloth in the room, led the gold-ass in and said to his brother “now, dear brother, speak with him.”
sentences.i: Then the turner spread a cloth in the parlour, led the gold-ass in, and said to his brother: “Now, dear brother, speak to him.”
sentences.n: “hereinführen” (to lead in) is separable: “führte… herein.” The turner lets each brother work his own recovered gift.

sentences.t: Der Müller sagte „Bricklebrit,“ und augenblicklich sprangen die Goldstücke auf das Tuch herab, als käme ein Platzregen, und der Esel hörte nicht eher auf als bis alle so viel hatten, daß sie nicht mehr tragen konnten.
sentences.l: The miller said ‘Bricklebrit,’ and instantly the gold pieces sprang down onto the cloth, as if a cloudburst were coming, and the ass did not leave off sooner than until all had so much that they could carry no more.
sentences.i: The miller said ‘Bricklebrit,’ and instantly the gold coins came showering down onto the cloth, as if a cloudburst had broken, and the ass did not stop until they all had so much they could carry no more.
sentences.n: der Platzregen = a downpour, cloudburst; käme is subjunctive in the “as if” clause (als käme = as if there came). “aufhören” (to stop, leave off) is separable: “hörte… auf.”

sentences.t: (Ich sehe dirs an, du wärst auch gerne dabei gewesen.)
sentences.l: (I see it on you, you would gladly have been there too.)
sentences.i: (I can see it in your face — you'd have liked to be there yourself.)
sentences.n: A direct aside to the reader, in parentheses. “einem etwas ansehen” = to tell something by looking at someone (“I can see it in you”); dirs = dir es. wärst… gewesen is subjunctive (would have been). “dabei sein” = to be there, be in on it.

sentences.t: Dann holte der Drechsler das Tischchen und sagte „lieber Bruder, nun sprich mit ihm.“
sentences.l: Then the turner fetched the little table and said “dear brother, now speak with it.”
sentences.i: Then the turner fetched the little table and said: “Dear brother, now speak to it.”

sentences.t: Und kaum hatte der Schreiner „Tischchen deck dich“ gesagt, so war es gedeckt und mit den schönsten Schüsseln reichlich besetzt.
sentences.l: And scarcely had the joiner said ‘little-table cover yourself,’ then it was covered and richly laden with the finest dishes.
sentences.i: And scarcely had the joiner said ‘Table, set yourself,’ than it was spread and richly laden with the finest dishes.
sentences.n: “kaum… so…” = scarcely… when… — an old correlative, with the verb fronted (“kaum hatte… gesagt”). reichlich = abundantly; besetzt = laden, covered.

sentences.t: Da ward eine Mahlzeit gehalten, wie der gute Schneider noch keine in seinem Hause erlebt hatte, und die ganze Verwandtschaft blieb beisammen bis in die Nacht, und waren alle lustig und vergnügt.
sentences.l: Then a meal was held such as the good tailor had never yet experienced in his house, and the whole kinfolk stayed together until into the night, and were all merry and content.
sentences.i: Then such a feast was held as the good tailor had never known in his house, and the whole family stayed together far into the night, all merry and content.
sentences.n: ward is the old past of werden (became/was) — here a passive: “ward gehalten” = was held. die Verwandtschaft = the kinfolk, relations. erleben = to live to see, experience.

sentences.t: Der Schneider verschloß Nadel und Zwirn, Elle und Bügeleisen in einen Schrank, und lebte mit seinen drei Söhnen in Freude und Herrlichkeit.
sentences.l: The tailor locked needle and thread, ell and flat-iron in a cupboard, and lived with his three sons in joy and splendor.
sentences.i: The tailor locked needle and thread, yardstick and flat-iron away in a cupboard, and lived with his three sons in joy and splendour.
sentences.n: verschließen = to lock away (past verschloß, 1857 ß). Bügeleisen = a flat-iron. He puts away every tool of his old drudgery — the tale's happy close, but not the end.

sentences.t: Wo ist aber die Ziege hingekommen, die Schuld war daß der Schneider seine drei Söhne fortjagte?
sentences.l: But where has the goat got to, who was the blame that the tailor chased off his three sons?
sentences.i: But whatever became of the goat, who was to blame for the tailor's driving off his three sons?
sentences.n: The tale circles back to its opening cause — the frame closing. “hingekommen” = got to, ended up. “Schuld sein, daß…” = to be to blame that…; fortjagen = to chase off.

sentences.t: Das will ich dir sagen.
sentences.l: That I will tell you.
sentences.i: That I'll tell you.
sentences.n: Again the storyteller turns straight to the listener (dir = you).

sentences.t: Sie schämte sich daß sie einen kahlen Kopf hatte, lief in eine Fuchshöhle und verkroch sich hinein.
sentences.l: She was ashamed that she had a bald head, ran into a fox's den and crept herself into it.
sentences.i: Ashamed of her bald head, she ran into a fox's den and hid herself away inside.
sentences.n: kahl = bald, bare (from the shaving). die Fuchshöhle = a fox's den. “sich verkriechen” = to creep away and hide (reflexive).

sentences.t: Als der Fuchs nach Haus kam, funkelten ihm ein paar große Augen aus der Dunkelheit entgegen, daß er erschrack und wieder zurücklief.
sentences.l: When the fox came home, a pair of big eyes sparkled toward him out of the darkness, so that he took fright and ran back again.
sentences.i: When the fox came home, a pair of big eyes gleamed at him out of the dark, so that he took fright and ran off again.
sentences.n: “entgegenfunkeln” = to sparkle/gleam toward (separable, dative ihm). erschrack is the old strong past of erschrecken (to take fright). zurücklaufen (to run back) is separable.

sentences.t: Der Bär begegnete ihm, und da der Fuchs ganz verstört aussah, so sprach er „was ist dir, Bruder Fuchs, was machst du für ein Gesicht?“
sentences.l: The bear met him, and since the fox looked quite distraught, then he spoke “what is with you, brother fox, what a face are you making?”
sentences.i: The bear met him, and since the fox looked utterly distraught, he said: “What's the matter, brother fox — why the long face?”
sentences.n: begegnen = to meet, encounter (takes the dative, ihm). verstört = distraught, bewildered. “was ist dir?” = what is the matter with you? “was machst du für ein Gesicht?” = what a face you're pulling.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ antwortete der Rothe, „ein grimmig Thier sitzt in meiner Höhle und hat mich mit feurigen Augen angeglotzt.“
sentences.l: “Ah,” answered the red one, “a fierce beast sits in my den and has goggled at me with fiery eyes.”
sentences.i: “Ah,” answered the red one, “a fierce beast is sitting in my den and glared at me with fiery eyes.”
sentences.n: der Rothe = “the red one,” i.e. the fox (from his colour; 1857 th- spelling of Rote). grimmig = fierce, ferocious. anglotzen = to gape/goggle at.

sentences.t: „Das wollen wir bald austreiben,“ sprach der Bär, gieng mit zu der Höhle und schaute hinein; als er aber die feurigen Augen erblickte, wandelte ihn ebenfalls Furcht an: er wollte mit dem grimmigen Thiere nichts zu thun haben und nahm Reißaus.
sentences.l: “That we will soon drive out,” spoke the bear, went along to the den and looked in; but when he caught sight of the fiery eyes, fear came over him likewise: he would have nothing to do with the fierce beast and took to his heels.
sentences.i: “We'll soon drive that out,” said the bear, went along to the den and looked in; but when he caught sight of the fiery eyes, fear came over him too: he wanted nothing to do with the fierce beast and took to his heels.
sentences.n: austreiben = to drive out, expel. “mitgehen” (to go along) separable: “gieng mit.” “Furcht wandelte ihn an” = fear came over him (“anwandeln” = to come over someone). Thiere keeps an old dative -e. “Reißaus nehmen” = to take to one's heels, bolt.

sentences.t: Die Biene begegnete ihm, und da sie merkte daß es ihm in seiner Haut nicht wohl zu Muthe war, sprach sie „Bär, du machst ja ein gewaltig verdrießlich Gesicht, wo ist deine Lustigkeit geblieben?“
sentences.l: The bee met him, and since she noticed that he was not well at ease in his skin, she spoke “bear, you are making such a mightily sullen face, where has your merriment gone?”
sentences.i: The bee met him, and seeing that he was ill at ease in his own skin, she said: “Bear, what a terribly glum face you're pulling — where's your good cheer gone?”
sentences.n: die Biene = bee. “einem ist wohl zu Muthe” = one feels at ease (Muthe = 1857 for Mute, mood, spirits). verdrießlich = sullen, glum. die Lustigkeit = merriment, cheerfulness; “wo ist… geblieben?” = where has it got to?

sentences.t: „Du hast gut reden,“ antwortete der Bär, „es sitzt ein grimmiges Thier mit Glotzaugen in dem Hause des Rothen, und wir können es nicht herausjagen.“
sentences.l: “You have good talking,” answered the bear, “a fierce beast with goggle-eyes sits in the house of the red one, and we cannot drive it out.”
sentences.i: “It's all very well for you to talk,” answered the bear; “there's a fierce beast with goggle-eyes sitting in the red one's house, and we can't drive it out.”
sentences.n: “du hast gut reden” = it's easy for you to talk. Glotzaugen = goggle-eyes, staring eyes. herausjagen = to drive out. “des Rothen” = of the red one (genitive of der Rothe, the fox).

sentences.t: Die Biene sprach „du dauerst mich, Bär, ich bin ein armes schwaches Geschöpf, das ihr im Wege nicht anguckt, aber ich glaube doch daß ich euch helfen kann.“
sentences.l: The bee spoke “you move me to pity, bear, I am a poor weak creature that you do not even look at in the way, but I believe nonetheless that I can help you.”
sentences.i: The bee said: “I'm sorry for you, bear. I'm a poor, weak creature that you wouldn't even glance at in the road — but I think I can help you all the same.”
sentences.n: “du dauerst mich” = I feel sorry for you (dauern here = to move to pity). das Geschöpf = creature. “im Wege” = in the way, on the road; angucken = to look at.

sentences.t: Sie flog in die Fuchshöhle, setzte sich der Ziege auf den glatten geschorenen Kopf, und stach sie so gewaltig, daß sie aufsprang, „meh! meh!“ schrie, und wie toll in die Welt hineinlief; und weiß niemand auf diese Stunde wo sie hingelaufen ist.
sentences.l: She flew into the fox's den, settled herself on the goat's smooth shorn head, and stung her so mightily, that she leaped up, screamed ‘baa! baa!’, and ran off into the world like mad; and nobody knows to this hour where she has run to.
sentences.i: She flew into the fox's den, settled on the goat's smooth, shorn head, and stung her so hard that she leapt up, screamed ‘baa! baa!’, and bolted off into the world like mad; and no one knows to this hour where she has run to.
sentences.n: “sich setzen” = to settle, alight. “der Ziege auf den Kopf” is dative-of-possession (onto the goat's head). geschoren = shorn (from scheren). aufspringen (to leap up) and hineinlaufen (to run in) are separable. “wie toll” = like mad; hingelaufen = run off (to where). The goat vanishes bald into the world — the frame's last, unresolved note.
